en.extract:
The pale-headed munia (Lonchura pallida) is a species of estrildid finch native to eastern Indonesia and Timor-Leste. It inhabits subtropical and tropical lowlands, dry shrubland, grassland, marshes and agricultural areas. The species has shown recent range expansion due to increasing agriculture and land clearance. It has recently crossed the Wallace Line to establish breeding populations on Nusa Penida, and is now recorded on Bali. It has also become an increasingly frequent vagrant in northwestern Australia from the 2020s onward, with arrivals likely aided by monsoon winds and cyclones.


Taxonomy
The species was first described by the British naturalist Alfred Russel Wallace in 1864 under the binomial Munia pallida, based on specimens from the Lesser Sunda Islands. Its type locality is generally given as Lombok and Flores. 
It is now placed in the genus Lonchura and is commonly regarded as having no subspecies. A population described as Lonchura pallida subcastanea from the Palu Valley of Sulawesi is generally considered synonymous with the nominate form.
The pale-headed munia belongs to a complex of pale-capped species within Lonchura, including the white-headed munia (L. maja), with which it is sometimes confused. 


Etymology
The genus name Lonchura combines the Ancient Greek words lonkhē (λόγχη, "spear-head" or "lance") and oura (οὐρά, "tail"), referring to the pointed or lance-shaped tail feathers characteristic of many munias. The species name pallida is Latin for "pale", alluding to the bird's diagnostically pale head plumage, which contrasts with the darker body and was noted in the species' original 1864 description. 


Description
The pale-headed munia is a small finch measuring approximately 10–11cm in length and weighing around 10–15g, with a striking white or pale buff head contrasting against a dark body. Adults are rich chestnut brown above, with a reddish-brown tail and a pale buff to peach-washed underside. The bill is pale blue-grey, the iris dark brown, and legs pale bluish-grey. Juveniles are much duller and browner overall but typically show a paler head. No significant sexual dimorphism is noted.


Similar species
Pale-headed munia can be distinguished from the white-headed munia by its reddish-brown rather than chestnut brown tail, tan-coloured rather than chestnut brown flanks, and the absence of the latter species' contrasting black or very dark brown belly patch. The pale-headed munia typically has a pale buff to creamy-white head, while the second species usually has a pure, snowy white head.
Juveniles differ from those of the five-coloured munia (L. quinticolor) by having a noticeably smaller bill and paler head.


Range and distribution
The species' historical range includes central and south Sulawesi, Kalaotoa, Madu, and the Lesser Sundas. It occurs in dry shrub and grassland habitats, including marshes, croplands, and scrub in the lowlands. Abundance varies: it is locally common in the north of Flores, uncommon on Sumba, and common on Lombok. 
The pale-headed munia was only recently added to the official list of birds resident on the island of Timor, where it is generally assumed to be indigenous, having possibly just evaded notice during previous official bird surveys. It was first formally recorded on the island in 1998.


Expansion
The species appears to be expanding on Sulawesi due to land clearance and farming.
While records have previously listed the species' westernmost limit as Lombok, it has apparently recently jumped the Wallace Line—a major biogeographic boundary separating Asian and Australasian fauna—and is now breeding on Nusa Penida, just off Bali. A 2008 local bird checklist omits the species and it is not present in other recent Bali-area guides. The timeline and mechanism of this apparent colonisation is unclear, but a bird was photographed there in March 2019 and a number of flocks including juveniles have been photographed on the island from at least 2023 onwards.
Birds were photographed near the Unda River mouth in eastern Bali in 2025, indicating possible recent colonisation of the island.


Australia
Vagrant pale-headed munias have increasingly been sighted in Western Australia, with records first beginning in the 2020s. A single individual was photographed at Arrow Pearling Base on the Dampier Peninsula in February 2022. There was a claimed sighting of another bird on Troughton Island on the Kimberley coast in December 2024, and at least nine birds were photographed on the same island in late January 2026, after landing there amid strong winds from Cyclone Luana. There is an approximate 450-600 kilometre distance between Troughton Island and the species' closest known populations, centring on Timor. 
The Troughton flock reportedly disappeared from the 0.93 km2 island after a few days, possibly dispersing to the nearby Australian mainland. Three pale headed munias were spotted on Troughton over a week after the initial group's disappearance. It is unknown if they were returnees from the original flock or new birds, but it is speculated they may have been assisted back to the island from the mainland by a southerly wind.
Prior to the Western Australian arrivals, the species' Australian records had been confined to sightings of vagrants approximately 320 kilometres off the Australian coast, on the country's external territory of the Ashmore and Cartier Islands. One group of three munias was recorded there in February 2000, and a pair was noted in January 2003.


Trends in vagrancy
Both single birds and flocks have been recorded in cases of extreme geographical dispersal beyond its established range. Australian records of pale-headed munias have occurred during the northwest wet season, which is often marked by monsoon or cyclonic winds, suggesting storm-assisted movement from Timor or other nearby islands. 


Behaviour and ecology
The species is highly gregarious, commonly travelling and feeding in large flocks, sometimes of up to 200 individuals, and often in the company of other small estrildids. It often roosts communally, gathering in vegetation or reed beds at dusk. 


Diet
Pale-headed munias are primarily seed-eaters, although some insects are taken. They forage on grasses and cultivated grains in open habitats, shrub lands, and modified landscapes such as rice fields. The species appears to have benefited from grain production in parts of Indonesia. 


Breeding
Breeding pairs go to nest during periods of high food availability, usually the wet season, building domed or globular nests concealed in shrubs or tall grass. A typical clutch consists of 4 to 6 white eggs, incubated by both parents for about 12 to 14 days. Chicks are fed regurgitated seeds and some insects by both parents, and fledge after approximately 21 days.


Vocalisations
The species produces weak, high-pitched "weee" contact calls and stronger, metallic "wit-wit-wit" notes, often given in flight or while foraging in flocks.


Conservation status
The pale-headed munia is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN. The global population has not been quantified but the species is described as common or stable within its range. Its very large range (extent of occurrence ≈1,130,000 km²) buffers it against threats. The species has locally benefited from agricultural expansion and land clearance, with no major declines or substantial threats identified. 


In captivity
It is kept and traded as a cage bird across its range in Indonesia, raising the potential for over-exploitation. Market surveys conducted in Mataram, Lombok in 2018–2019 recorded 130 individuals offered for sale across multiple visits, making it one of the more abundant species on sale. All observed trade was technically illegal, as no harvest quotas existed for the species at the time; most birds appeared to be locally sourced rather than part of large-scale national or international supply chains.
The species is sometimes offered for sale in Europe and North America, where it has been bred in captivity using standard munia husbandry techniques, but it is not as widely known or traded as the white headed munia. It is held by some European zoos, such as Plzen Zoo in the Czech Republic. The species is not believed to be present in captivity in Australia, where new imports of exotic estrildids are presently illegal.
No large-scale captive-breeding programs are known, and the species is not part of any formal ex-situ conservation efforts.


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet

The '''pale-headed munia''' (''Lonchura pallida'') is a species of [[estrildid finch]] native to eastern [[Indonesia]] and [[Timor-Leste]]. It inhabits subtropical and tropical lowlands, dry [[shrubland]], [[grassland]], [[Marsh|marshes]] and [[Agricultural land|agricultural]] areas. The species has shown recent range expansion due to increasing agriculture and land clearance. It has recently crossed the [[Wallace Line]] to establish breeding populations on [[Nusa Penida]], and is now recorded on [[Bali]]. It has also become an increasingly frequent [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]] in northwestern [[Australia]] from the 2020s onward, with arrivals likely aided by [[monsoon]] winds and [[Cyclone|cyclones]].

== Taxonomy ==
The species was first described by the [[British people|British]] naturalist [[Alfred Russel Wallace]] in 1864 under the [[Binomial nomenclature|binomial]] ''Munia pallida'', based on specimens from the [[Lesser Sunda Islands|Lesser Sunda]] Islands.<ref name=":4">Wallace, A. R. (1864). "List of Birds from Timor, Flores, and Sumbawa, with Descriptions of New Species". ''Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London'' (for 1863, part 4, published 1864): 480–485 [specifically page [https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30681855 495] for ''Munia pallida'']</ref> Its type locality is generally given as [[Lombok]] and [[Flores]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-07-27 |title=Pale-headed Munia / Lonchura pallida |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.com/bird/pale-headed-munia/28359.html |access-date=2026-03-07 |website=World Bird Names |language=en-US}}</ref> 

It is now placed in the genus ''[[Lonchura]]'' and is commonly regarded as having no [[subspecies]]. A population described as ''Lonchura pallida subcastanea'' from the Palu Valley of [[Sulawesi]] is generally considered synonymous with the nominate form.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Pale-headed Munia - BirdForum Opus |url=https://www.birdforum.net/opus/Pale-headed_Munia |access-date=2026-03-07 |website=BirdForum |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>Cornell Lab of Ornithology (2024). "Updates and Corrections—October 2024". <nowiki>''</nowiki>eBird/Clements Checklist of Birds of the World<nowiki>''</nowiki>. Retrieved 7 March 2026, from <nowiki>https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/updates-and-corrections-october-2024/</nowiki></ref>

The pale-headed munia belongs to a [[Species complex|complex]] of pale-capped species within ''Lonchura'', including the [[white-headed munia]] (''L. maja''), with which it is sometimes confused.<ref>Payne, Robert B. (2024). "Pale-headed Munia (''Lonchura pallida''), version 1.1". In: ''Birds of the World'' (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, E. de Juana, and S. M. Billerman, eds.). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. <nowiki>https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.pahmun1.01.1</nowiki></ref> 

== Etymology ==
The genus name ''Lonchura'' combines the [[Ancient Greek]] words ''lonkhē'' (λόγχη, "spear-head" or "lance") and ''oura'' (οὐρά, "tail"), referring to the pointed or lance-shaped tail feathers characteristic of many munias.<ref>Sykes, William Henry (1832). "Catalogue of birds of the raptorial and insessorial orders (systematically arranged,) observed in the Dukhun". ''Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London''. '''2''' (18): 77–99 [94]. <nowiki>https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12861692</nowiki>.</ref> The species name ''pallida'' is Latin for "pale",<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=289 }}</ref> alluding to the bird's diagnostically pale head plumage, which contrasts with the darker body and was noted in the species' original 1864 description.<ref name=":4" /> 

== Description ==
The pale-headed munia is a small finch measuring approximately 10–11cm in length and weighing around 10–15g, with a striking white or pale buff head contrasting against a dark body. Adults are rich chestnut brown above, with a reddish-brown tail and a pale buff to peach-washed underside. The bill is pale blue-grey, the iris dark brown, and legs pale bluish-grey. Juveniles are much duller and browner overall but typically show a paler head. No significant sexual dimorphism is noted.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Pale-headed Munia - eBird|url=https://ebird.org/species/pahmun1?siteLanguage=en_AU|website=ebird.org|access-date=2026-01-29|archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20241214015947/https://ebird.org/species/pahmun1?siteLanguage=en_AU|archive-date=2024-12-14|language=en}}</ref>

=== Similar species ===
Pale-headed munia can be distinguished from the white-headed munia by its reddish-brown rather than chestnut brown tail, tan-coloured rather than chestnut brown flanks, and the absence of the latter species' contrasting black or very dark brown belly patch. The pale-headed munia typically has a pale buff to creamy-white head, while the second species usually has a pure, snowy white head.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=White-headed Munia - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/species/whhmun1 |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20251130204759/https://ebird.org/species/whhmun1 |archive-date=2025-11-30 |access-date=2026-03-21 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref>

Juveniles differ from those of the [[five-coloured munia]] (''L. quinticolor'') by having a noticeably smaller bill and paler head.<ref name=":2" />

== Range and distribution ==
The species' historical range includes central and south Sulawesi, Kalaotoa, Madu, and the Lesser Sundas. It occurs in dry shrub and grassland habitats, including marshes, croplands, and scrub in the lowlands. Abundance varies: it is locally common in the north of Flores, uncommon on [[Sumba]], and common on Lombok.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Payne |first=Robert B. |date=2024 |title=Pale-headed Munia (Lonchura pallida), version 1.1 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/pahmun1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.pahmun1.01.1 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref> 

The pale-headed munia was only recently added to the official list of birds resident on the island of [[Timor]], where it is generally assumed to be indigenous, having possibly just evaded notice during previous official bird surveys.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Pinto|first=Pedro|date=2008-01-01|title=Birds, birding and conservation in Timor-Leste|url=https://www.academia.edu/18850040/Birds_birding_and_conservation_in_Timor_Leste|journal=BirdingASIA|volume=9 (2008)}}</ref><ref name=":0" /> It was first formally recorded on the island in 1998.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Arti penting hutan di daratan Timor bagian barat: telaah awal informasi keanekaragaman hayati dan sosial ekonomi di Pulau Timor (Propinsi Nusa Tenggara Timur)|last1=Lesmana |first1=D. |last2=Trainor |first2=C. |last3=Gatur |first3=A. |date=2000|language=id |trans-title=The importance of forest areas in West Timor: a review of biodiversity and socio-economic information based on rapid surveys}}</ref>

=== Expansion ===
The species appears to be expanding on Sulawesi due to land clearance and farming.<ref name=":0" />

While records have previously listed the species' westernmost limit as Lombok, it has apparently recently jumped the Wallace Line—a major [[Biogeography|biogeographic]] boundary separating [[Asia|Asian]] and [[Australasia|Australasian]] fauna—and is now breeding on Nusa Penida, just off Bali.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/checklist.jsp?region=IDlsba02&list=howardmoore|title=Avibase - Bird Checklists of the World: Nusa Penida|date=2026|website=Avibase - The World Bird Database|last=Lepage|first=Denis}}</ref> A 2008 local bird checklist omits the species<ref>{{Cite web|title=Burung-burung di Bali & Nusa Penida dalam|url=https://kabarburungkibc.wordpress.com/edisi-november-2008/burung-burung-di-bali-nusa-penida-dalam/|website=Kabar-kabar burung di mana-mana|date=2008-12-11|access-date=2026-02-02|language=id-ID}}</ref> and it is not present in other recent Bali-area guides.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Birds|url=https://baliwildlife.com/encyclopedia/encyclopedia-category/animals/birds/|website=Bali Wildlife|access-date=2026-02-02|language=en-US}}</ref> The timeline and mechanism of this apparent colonisation is unclear, but a bird was photographed there in March 2019<ref>{{Cite web|title=Pale-headed Munia (Lonchura pallida)|url=https://inaturalist.ala.org.au/observations/38793290|website=iNaturalist Australia|date=2020-02-18|access-date=2026-02-02|language=en|first=Hans|last=Ohrt}}</ref> and a number of flocks including juveniles have been photographed on the island from at least 2023 onwards.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-06-21 |title=ML608319641 - Pale-headed Munia - Macaulay Library |url=https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/608319627 |access-date=2026-03-18 |website=macaulaylibrary.org}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2024-10-15 |title=ML624983730 - Pale-headed Munia - Macaulay Library |url=https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/624983730 |access-date=2026-03-18 |website=macaulaylibrary.org}}</ref>

Birds were photographed near the Unda River mouth in eastern Bali in 2025, indicating possible recent colonisation of the island.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-11-28 |title=ML646225514 - Pale-headed Munia - Macaulay Library |url=https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/646225514 |access-date=2026-03-18 |website=macaulaylibrary.org}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2025-11-28 |title=ML646225462 - Pale-headed Munia - Macaulay Library |url=https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/646225462 |access-date=2026-03-18 |website=macaulaylibrary.org}}</ref>

=== Australia ===
Vagrant pale-headed munias have increasingly been sighted in [[Western Australia]], with records first beginning in the 2020s. A single individual was photographed at Arrow Pearling Base on the [[Dampier Peninsula]] in February 2022.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-02-13 |title=ML415688621 - Pale-headed Munia - Macaulay Library |url=https://macaulaylibrary.org/asset/415688621 |access-date=2026-03-09 |website=macaulaylibrary.org}}</ref><ref name=":3">{{Cite web|title=Memorandum to:|url=https://archive.birdlife.org.au/documents/barc/SUMM1356.htm|website=archive.birdlife.org.au|access-date=2026-01-31}}</ref> There was a claimed sighting of another bird on [[Troughton Island]] on the [[Kimberley coastline (Western Australia)|Kimberley coast]] in December 2024,<ref>{{Cite web |title=WA Bird Notes 194 June 2025 |url=https://www.calameo.com/books/0041078958f305aa6b0ed |access-date=2026-01-29 |website=calameo.com |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Memorandum to:|url=https://archive.birdlife.org.au/documents/barc/SUMM1355.htm|website=archive.birdlife.org.au|access-date=2026-01-31}}</ref> and at least nine birds were photographed on the same island in late January 2026, after landing there amid strong winds from [[2025–26 Australian region cyclone season#Tropical Cyclone Luana|Cyclone Luana]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Frogley |first=Matt |date=2026-01-28 |title=Pale-headed Munia (Lonchura pallida) |url=https://inaturalist.ala.org.au/observations/336453211 |access-date=2026-01-29 |website=iNaturalist Australia |language=en}}</ref> There is an approximate 450-600 kilometre distance between Troughton Island and the species' closest known populations, centring on Timor. 

The Troughton flock reportedly disappeared from the 0.93 km<sup>2</sup> island after a few days, possibly dispersing to the nearby [[Mainland Australia|Australian mainland]].<ref name=":1" /> Three pale headed munias were spotted on Troughton over a week after the initial group's disappearance. It is unknown if they were returnees from the original flock or new birds, but it is speculated they may have been assisted back to the island from the mainland by a southerly wind.<ref name=":1" />

Prior to the Western Australian arrivals, the species' Australian records had been confined to sightings of vagrants approximately 320 kilometres off the Australian coast, on the country's [[External territory of Australia|external territory]] of the [[Ashmore and Cartier Islands]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Ashmore Reef and Cartier Island bird checklist - Avibase - Bird Checklists of the World |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/checklist.jsp?region=AUac&list=howardmoore |access-date=2026-01-29 |website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref> One group of three munias was recorded there in February 2000, and a pair was noted in January 2003.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Memorandum to:|url=https://archive.birdlife.org.au/documents/barc/SUMM296.htm|website=archive.birdlife.org.au|access-date=2026-01-31}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Memorandum to:|url=https://archive.birdlife.org.au/documents/barc/SUMM433.htm|website=archive.birdlife.org.au|access-date=2026-01-31}}</ref>

=== Trends in vagrancy ===
Both single birds and flocks have been recorded in cases of extreme geographical dispersal beyond its established range. Australian records of pale-headed munias have occurred during the northwest wet season, which is often marked by [[monsoon]] or cyclonic winds, suggesting storm-assisted movement from Timor or other nearby islands.<ref name=":3" /><ref name=":1" /> 

== Behaviour and ecology ==
The species is highly [[Sociality|gregarious]], commonly travelling and feeding in large flocks, sometimes of up to 200 individuals, and often in the company of other small estrildids. It often roosts communally, gathering in vegetation or [[reed bed]]s at dusk.<ref name=":0" /> 

=== Diet ===
Pale-headed munias are primarily [[Seed predation|seed-eaters]], although some insects are taken. They forage on grasses and cultivated grains in open habitats, shrub lands, and modified landscapes such as [[Paddy field|rice fields]]. The species appears to have benefited from grain production in parts of Indonesia.<ref name=":2" /><ref name="birdlife">{{Cite web |title=Pale-headed Munia ''Lonchura pallida'' Species Factsheet |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/pale-headed-munia-lonchura-pallida |access-date=2026-03-07 |website=BirdLife DataZone |language=en}}</ref> 

=== Breeding ===
Breeding pairs go to nest during periods of high food availability, usually the [[wet season]], building domed or globular nests concealed in shrubs or tall grass. A typical clutch consists of 4 to 6 white eggs, incubated by both parents for about 12 to 14 days. Chicks are fed regurgitated seeds and some insects by both parents, and [[fledge]] after approximately 21 days.<ref name=":0" />

=== Vocalisations ===
The species produces weak, high-pitched "weee" contact calls and stronger, metallic "wit-wit-wit" notes, often given in flight or while foraging in flocks.<ref name=":2" />

== Conservation status ==
The pale-headed munia is classified as [[Least-concern species|Least Concern]] by the [[IUCN]]. The global population has not been quantified but the species is described as common or stable within its range.<ref name=":5" /> Its very large range (extent of occurrence ≈1,130,000 km²) buffers it against threats. The species has locally benefited from agricultural expansion and land clearance, with no major declines or substantial threats identified.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last=BirdLife International (BirdLife International) |date=2018-08-09 |title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Lonchura pallida |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22719854/132132720 |journal=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species |archive-url=http://web.archive.org/web/20250224085020/https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22719854/132132720 |archive-date=2025-02-24}}</ref> 

== In captivity ==
It is kept and traded as a [[Cage-bird trade|cage bird]] across its range in Indonesia, raising the potential for over-exploitation. Market surveys conducted in [[Mataram (city)|Mataram]], Lombok in 2018–2019 recorded 130 individuals offered for sale across multiple visits, making it one of the more abundant species on sale. All observed trade was technically illegal, as no harvest quotas existed for the species at the time; most birds appeared to be locally sourced rather than part of large-scale national or international supply chains.<ref>Leupen, B.T.C., Shepherd, L., Shepherd, C.R., Damianou, E., & Nijman, V. (2022). Market surveys in Mataram, Lombok. ''Indonesian Journal of Applied Environmental Studies'' 3(1), 42–52.</ref>

The species is sometimes offered for sale in Europe and North America, where it has been bred in captivity using standard munia husbandry techniques, but it is not as widely known or traded as the white headed munia.<ref>Griffiths, A. (1986). Breeding the Pale-headed Mannikin Lonchura pallida. Avicult. Mag. 92(2): 64-65.</ref><ref>Payne, R.B. (2024). Pale-headed Munia (''Lonchura pallida''). In ''Birds of the World'' (Cornell Lab of Ornithology).</ref> It is held by some European zoos, such as [[Plzeň Zoo|Plzen Zoo]] in the [[Czech Republic]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=BIR039-00158 |url=https://www.joelsartore.com/bir039-00158/ |access-date=2026-03-22 |website=Joel Sartore |language=en-US}}</ref> The species is not believed to be present in captivity in Australia, where new imports of exotic estrildids are presently illegal.<ref>Australian Government, Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water (DCCEEW). ''Live Import List''. Last updated 27 January 2026. <nowiki>https://www.dcceew.gov.au/environment/wildlife-trade/live-import-list</nowiki>. "If a specimen is not in the Live Import List then it cannot be imported". Accessed 22 March 2026.</ref><ref>United Bird Societies of South Australia Inc. (2020). ''Bird Price Guide 2020 Edition''. Port Augusta, South Australia: United Bird Societies of South Australia Inc. Available at: <nowiki>https://unitedbirds.asn.au/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Bird-Price-Guide-2020-Edition.pdf</nowiki> </ref>

No large-scale captive-breeding programs are known, and the species is not part of any formal [[Ex situ conservation|ex-situ conservation]] efforts.<ref name="birdlife"/>

zh.extract:
淡色文鸟（学名：Lonchura pallida），是梅花雀科文鸟属的一种鸟类，其种加词“pallida ”意为“淡色的”。分布于东帝汶和印度尼西亚。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
淡色文鸟的栖息地包括亚热带或热带的（低地）干草原、耕地、亚热带或热带的（低地）干燥疏灌丛和牧草地。


参考文献

Lonchura pallida. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27]. （原始内容存档于2016-03-04）.
